{{Short description="Force equals the rate of change of momentum}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Newton's second law
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Newton's laws of motion]], [[Force]], [[Momentum]], [[Mass]], [[Acceleration]]
| formula = <math>\mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt}</math>
}}

'''Newton's second law''' of motion states that the [[force]] acting on an object is equal to the rate of change of its [[momentum]]. In its most general form:

:<math>\mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt}</math>

where <math>\mathbf{F}</math> is the net force and <math>\mathbf{p} = m\mathbf{v}</math> is the momentum.

For a system with constant [[mass]], this simplifies to the more familiar form:

:<math>\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}</math>

where <math>m</math> is the mass and <math>\mathbf{a}</math> is the [[acceleration]].

== Significance ==
Newton's second law is the foundation of [[classical mechanics]] and [[dynamics (mechanics)|dynamics]]. It:
* Defines the quantitative relationship between force, mass, and acceleration
* Introduces the concept of [[mass|inertial mass]] as resistance to acceleration
* Provides the basis for equations of motion
* Implies [[conservation of momentum]] for isolated systems

== Vector nature ==
Force and acceleration are [[vector (physics)|vectors]] — the direction of acceleration matches the direction of the net force. In component form:

:<math>F_x = m a_x,\quad F_y = m a_y,\quad F_z = m a_z</math>

== Variable mass systems ==
For systems where mass changes (e.g., a [[rocket]] expelling fuel), the second law must be applied to the total system:

:<math>m\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}}\frac{dm}{dt}</math>

where <math>\mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}}</math> is the exhaust velocity relative to the rocket.

== Impulse–momentum theorem ==
Integrating Newton's second law over time:

:<math>\mathbf{J} = \int\mathbf{F}\,dt = \Delta\mathbf{p}</math>

== Newton's other laws ==
* [[Newton's first law]] — [[inertia]]: an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net force
* [[Newton's third law]] — action and reaction: <math>\mathbf{F}_{12} = -\mathbf{F}_{21}</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Acceleration]]
* [[Force]]
* [[Mass]]
* [[Momentum]]
* [[Newton's laws of motion]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Newton's laws of motion]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Equations of physics]]
